original_abstract:
In the field of image processing, a diverse range of methodologies has been developed to address critical tasks such as denoising, enhancement, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification. These techniques collectively overcome the multifaceted challenges of image analysis, enabling transformative applications across various scientific and industrial domains. Modern image analysis is primarily defined by the divergence between traditional image processing—which relies on handcrafted algorithms and predefined heuristics—and Deep Learning (DL) models, which automatically learn intricate feature representations directly from raw data. 

Through a comparative lens, this review evaluates the performance and trade-offs of leading architectures. In denoising, models such as Self2Self NN, DnCNN, DFT-Net, and MPR-CNN offer significant noise reduction but remain sensitive to data augmentation strategies and parameter tuning. Image enhancement frameworks like R2R and LE-Net demonstrate a capacity for improving visual quality, yet maintaining authenticity in complex, real-world scenes remains a persistent hurdle. Similarly, segmentation benchmarks such as PSPNet and Mask R-CNN provide high precision in object isolation, though they face ongoing challenges regarding computational robustness and the handling of overlapping structures. For feature extraction and classification, architectures ranging from standard CNNs to hybrid CNN-LSTM networks highlight the power of automated recognition, while simultaneously raising concerns regarding interpretability and high computational overhead. By synthesizing these developments, this review provides a comprehensive understanding of the current state of the art, highlighting the specific strengths and limitations that govern the selection of methodologies in practical visual data analysis.
humanized_abstract:
Image processing is a field which employs a large variety of techniques in order to apply the desired operations, such as noise reduction, illumination adjustment, objects separation, feature analysis, and image classification. These techniques deal with the multifaceted aspects of the image analysis and thus enable the accomplishment of highly important scientific and industrial applications. Image analysis, as it is practiced today, has undergone a major transition between classical image processing and Deep Learning (DL) models, where the classical approach relies heavily on prior knowledge such as manually designed image features and domain specific assumptions, while the models used in the context of DL, are capable of learning the more complex features directly from the data themselves.

This review explores the existing state-of-the-art models for visual data analysis through comparative studies of their performance and characteristics. The models used in denoising, image enhancement, and segmentation are listed as follows. In the denoising task, the Self2Self NN, DnCNN, DFT-Net and MPR-CNN models can effectively reduce the noise in images but are sensitive to the data augmentation technique and hyperparameters. The R2R and LE-Net image enhancement frameworks can improve the visual quality of images, while maintaining the authenticity of the enhanced images, especially for complicated backgrounds in real-world images. The models in the segmentation task such as PSPNet and Mask R-CNN can achieve a very high accuracy in identifying the objects of interest in images, while suffering from low efficiency, instability, and poor robustness against the touching objects. The models for feature learning and image classification include the common used CNNs and CNN-LSTM networks. They can achieve a strong capability for the automatic feature learning and image classification, but also suffer from poor interpretability and high complexity. Finally, this review draws an overall conclusion of the current state-of-the-art models for visual data analysis by integrating the characteristics, comparative studies, and selections of the models used in different visual data analysis tasks.
